title: Cedar Plank Salmon with Pinot Gris Glaze & Lemon Garlic Yogurt Sauce
description: Cedar plank salmon soaked in pinot gris, finished with a wine-honey glaze and served with a lemon garlic yogurt sauce.

instructions.text: Submerge cedar plank in pinot gris, topping up with water as needed. Weigh it down so it stays submerged. Soak at least 1 hour, 2 hours preferred. Reserve the soaking liquid when ready to cook. Set salmon out to approach room temperature.
Pat salmon fillets dry with paper towels. Mix salt and brown sugar and apply a light coating to the flesh side only — not the skin. Set on a plate uncovered on the counter.

Combine pinot gris and honey in a small saucepan over medium heat. Reduce until syrupy, about 5–6 minutes. Remove from heat, stir in butter and dill or tarragon if using. Set aside — it will thicken as it cools. If it seizes up, thin with a few drops of warm water.
Combine yogurt, lemon juice, minced garlic, and olive oil in a small bowl. Thin with water to drizzle consistency. Season with a pinch of salt, taste and adjust. Set aside.
Preheat grill on all burners to medium-high, lid closed, target 400°F.

Remove plank from soaking liquid — reserve the liquid. Place plank on grill over direct heat, lid closed. Wait until it starts smoking and you can smell the cedar, about 3–4 minutes. Keep a spray bottle of water nearby the entire time. Once smoking, turn off the burner(s) directly under the plank — indirect heat from here on.

Pat salmon dry quickly and place skin-side down on the smoking plank with space between fillets. Brush flesh with first coat of glaze. Close grill lid and keep it closed. Watch for flare-ups; if the plank catches flame, spritz immediately with water.

Check salmon — flesh should be opaque about ¾ of the way up the side and just starting to flake when pressed lightly. Brush second coat of glaze. Optionally brush a small amount of reserved soaking liquid over flesh for a lacquered finish. Close lid 1–2 more minutes max. Target 125–130°F internal for medium, 145°F fully cooked through.

notes:
Thin fillets: pull at 90% done — residual heat finishes them. The window between perfect and overcooked is about 90 seconds.

Plank: heavy smoke is normal. Open flame is not — spritz and monitor throughout.

Glaze: if it seized and got too thick, thin with a few drops of warm water before applying the second coat.

Plank soak: minimum 1 hour in pinot gris, 2 hours preferred. Reserve the soaking liquid — a brush of it over the fish before the second glaze adds a lacquered finish.
